title: Practice Midterm Q2 — Recursion Tree for $T(n) = 3T(n/5) + n$ (4 pts)
statement:
Consider $T(n) = 3T(n/5) + n$, with $T(1) = 1$. Draw the recursion tree and use it to calculate $T(n)$ up to big-$\Theta$. Include:

- For a general level $i$: number of subproblems at that level, size of each subproblem.
- Total non-recursive work done at level $i$.
- Height of the tree, number of leaves, total work at the leaves.
- Your summing / upper bounding of the series.
- Conclude $T(n) = \Theta(?)$.

steps.title: Step 1 — Identify the parameters.
steps.body: We have $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with $a = 3$, $b = 5$, $f(n) = n$. At each level we want the subproblem count, size, and non-recursive work.

steps.title: Step 2 — Per-level accounting.
steps.body:
At level $i$ (root is level 0):

- **Number of subproblems:** $a^i = 3^i$.
- **Size of each subproblem:** $n / b^i = n / 5^i$.
- **Non-recursive work per subproblem:** $f(n/5^i) = n/5^i$.
- **Total non-recursive work at level $i$:** $3^i \cdot (n/5^i) = n \cdot (3/5)^i$.

This is a **decreasing geometric** with ratio $3/5 < 1$ — level work shrinks going down the tree.

tips.label: Exponent rule
tips.content: $a^i \cdot b^{-i} = (a/b)^i$. This is how the level-work $3^i \cdot (1/5)^i$ becomes $(3/5)^i$.

steps.title: Step 3 — Height and number of leaves.
steps.body:
Tree stops when subproblem size reaches $1$: $n/5^i = 1 \Rightarrow i = \log_5 n$. So the tree has $\log_5 n$ internal levels, plus leaves at depth $\log_5 n$.

Number of leaves: $3^{\log_5 n} = n^{\log_5 3}$ (using $a^{\log_b n} = n^{\log_b a}$).

Work at the leaves: each leaf is a base case $T(1) = 1$, so total leaf work $= n^{\log_5 3}$. Since $\log_5 3 \approx 0.68 < 1$, this is $o(n)$.

tips.label: Useful log identity
tips.content: $a^{\log_b n} = n^{\log_b a}$. Proof: take $\log_b$ of both sides, you get $(\log_b n) \log_b a$ on each side. This is how number-of-leaves turns into a power of $n$.

steps.title: Step 4 — Sum up.
steps.body:
Total runtime = internal-level work + leaf work:

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_5 n - 1} n \left(\tfrac{3}{5}\right)^i \;+\; n^{\log_5 3}.$$

For the geometric sum, upper-bound by the infinite series:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \left(\tfrac{3}{5}\right)^i = \dfrac{1}{1 - 3/5} = \dfrac{5}{2}.$$

So the first term is at most $\tfrac{5}{2} n = \Theta(n)$. Lower bound: the $i=0$ term alone is $n$, so the sum is at least $n$.

Leaves contribute $n^{\log_5 3} = O(n^{0.68})$, which is $o(n)$ and absorbed into $\Theta(n)$.

tips.label: Why $n^{\log_5 3}$ is negligible
tips.content: $\log_5 3 < 1$, so $n^{\log_5 3} = o(n)$. Polynomials beat polynomials with smaller exponents, no matter the log factors.
